what_it_does: This noticeboard block proposes a two-axis framework for judging whether an Obsidian feature can safely work with the project's ledger items. It explains that flat frontmatter is readable by Obsidian as-is, while custom address links in YAML (board://, image://, code://, decision://, session://) are invisible to Obsidian unless converted to [[wikilinks]]/file-paths, and it separates read-only features from write features because writes risk a two-writer conflict.
observations: The file is a noticeboard item of type block with id item-1d03e344, source claude_code, state current, channel dragnet-development.
The body defines Axis 1: whether Obsidian reads project data as-is or needs edges to be wikilinks/file-paths.
Project items have flat frontmatter fields (type, state, channel, author_session, created, etc.) plus a links list with kind and target address resolved through a single resolver.
Obsidian only registers links when it sees [[item-xxxx]] wikilinks; the YAML target strings are inert to Obsidian.
Axis 2 distinguishes read-only features from write features; writes reintroduce two-writer conflict between the cc_board runtime and Obsidian/Sync.
The title labels the item as P2.
standouts: The custom address resolver abstraction (board://, image://, code://, decision://, session://) and the claim that Obsidian cannot see those YAML links.
The read/write boundary is treated as the safety line for avoiding host-not-master conflicts.
conventions: Uses YAML frontmatter with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, created, updated, and history.
Uses Markdown body with a top-level heading and bold axis labels.
References code-style identifiers in backticks and uses board:// addressing for links.
Follows the noticeboard item structure with a part_of relation to a parent board item.
concerns: The claim that Obsidian cannot resolve the YAML address links is stated as absolute, without considering plugins, dataview, or custom code that could interpret them.
The phrase 'sort everything' is broad; the framework may not cover features that both read frontmatter and write metadata.
'Frame-risky' is used without definition in this file.
notes: A design lens that sorts Obsidian integration questions by how links are represented and whether the feature writes data.
questions: Which specific Obsidian features are being evaluated against these axes?
Where and how is the 'one resolver' for board://, image://, code://, decision://, session:// implemented?
What is the parent item board://item-3480c157?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to provide two axes that sort everything, and the file does present two axes for classifying Obsidian feature compatibility; they match at the level of a design framework.
apparent_themes: Host-not-master boundary between project runtime and Obsidian.
Canonical address resolution outside Obsidian's link graph.
Data model portability and representation mismatch.
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to establish a simple, reusable rule set for deciding which Obsidian features can be used as a read-only lens over the ledger and which should be avoided because they would compete with the project's own writer.
novelty: It treats the project's custom-resolved YAML addresses as the canonical relational layer and explicitly contrasts them with Obsidian's file-based wikilink graph, making the resolver abstraction central to integration analysis.
contribution: Provides a compact two-axis evaluation frame that a fused system could keep to decide which Obsidian features are safe to use and which risk misreading ledger links or creating write conflicts.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard design note that classifies Obsidian feature compatibility along two axes: whether Obsidian can read project data natively or needs wikilinks/file-paths, and whether the feature is read-only or write-capable.
intention_for_embedding: To create a simple decision rule for safely using Obsidian as a lens over the project's ledger without introducing two-writer conflicts.
